coredata.dc:title: Biowaste-derived multifunctional CeO2@carbon dot nanospheres for efficient sono-catalytic degradation of Rhodamine B dye and electrochemical sensing of 4-nitrophenol

Kaushal, Indu
Sharma, Ashok K.
Sharma, Sourabh
Kumar, Ashok
Bhukal, Santosh
Rani, Jyoti
Saharan, Priya
coredata.dc:description: Agro-biowastes, such as plant residues, vegetable scraps, and kitchen waste, are often discarded, leading to environmental pollution. This study focuses on transforming agro-waste into value-added Cerium Oxide@Carbon dot (CeO2@C-dots) hollow nanospheres, which serve as an efficient catalyst for the sono-catalytic degradation of Rhodamine B dye. The exceptional ultrasonic-assisted degradation performance of the material is attributed to the synergistic effect of carbon dots (C-dots) dispersed on the surface of CeO2 nanospheres. This synergy enhances dye adsorption and facilitates effective electron-hole pair separation, thanks to the material’s large surface area. Additionally, CeO2@C-dots nanospheres were employed as electron mediators in fabricating a robust, highly sensitive chemical sensor to detect 4-Nitrophenol (4NP) in aqueous solutions. The sensor exhibited excellent sensitivity, with a response of 43.4 μA/μMcm2 and a detection limit of 0.94 μM. These findings underscore the multifunctional potential of CeO2@C-dots nanospheres for both the removal of organic pollutants and environmental monitoring of 4NP.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
CeO2 nanoparticles
Biowaste
Rhodamine dye
Ultrasonic degradation
Nitrophenol sensing

Effect of catalyst dosage and dye concentrations

Plausible mechanism of RB dye degradation
Electrochemical performance of prepared CeO2@C-dotsnanospheremodified Au electrode towards 4- nitrophenol

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FTIR; (b); XRD of CeO2@C-dots nanosphere; FESEM of (c) CeO2NPs and (d) CeO2@C-dots nanosphere (inset shows hollow capped spheres).
TEM images of (a) C-dots and (d) CeO2@C-dotsnanospheres (inset shows hollow capped spheres).
(a) XPS survey spectrum of CeO2@C-dots nanosphere, (b)–(d) core level XPS spectra of Ce 3d, O 1 s, and C 1 s.
(a) Percentage removal efficiency; (b) UV vis spectra of RB dye at different conditions.
Effect of catalyst dose (a) and pH (b) at different dye concentration; (c) Effect of radical ion scavengers; (d) Rate kinetic study for the degradation of RB.
Proposed mechanism of RB dye degradation via sonocatalysis.
(a) Electrochemical 3 cell work station; (b) Cyclic voltammograms of 4NP (50 μM) pure Au and CeO2@C-dots hybridized Au electrode.
(a) I–V response of 4NPat different scan rates and (b) Plot of peak current vs. square root of scan rate.
Amperometric response at CeO2@C-dotsmodified Au electrodefor the detection of 4NP; inset shows calibration curveat different concentrations of 4NP.
Degradation mechanism of RB via two pathways: N-de-ethylation and chromophore cleavage.
Pseudo-first-order kinetic constants for sonosorption of RB.
Comparison of percentage removal ability of green metal oxide/carbon dots-based nanocomposite.

entry.bold: Metal oxide/carbon dots based green nanocomposite

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the reported CeO2@C-dots nanosphere electrochemical sensors with other similar nanocomposites for 4NP detection.

head.ce:title: Biowaste-derived multifunctional CeO2@carbon dot nanospheres for efficient sono-catalytic degradation of Rhodamine B dye and electrochemical sensing of 4-nitrophenol

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Agro-biowastes, such as plant residues, vegetable scraps, and kitchen waste, are often discarded, leading to environmental pollution. This study focuses on transforming agro-waste into value-added Cerium Oxide@Carbon dot (CeO2@C-dots) hollow nanospheres, which serve as an efficient catalyst for the sono-catalytic degradation of Rhodamine B dye. The exceptional ultrasonic-assisted degradation performance of the material is attributed to the synergistic effect of carbon dots (C-dots) dispersed on the surface of CeO2 nanospheres. This synergy enhances dye adsorption and facilitates effective electron-hole pair separation, thanks to the material’s large surface area. Additionally, CeO2@C-dots nanospheres were employed as electron mediators in fabricating a robust, highly sensitive chemical sensor to detect 4-Nitrophenol (4NP) in aqueous solutions. The sensor exhibited excellent sensitivity, with a response of 43.4 μA/μMcm2 and a detection limit of 0.94 μM. These findings underscore the multifunctional potential of CeO2@C-dots nanospheres for both the removal of organic pollutants and environmental monitoring of 4NP.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Environmental degradation due to the rapid expansion of industries such as pharmaceuticals, textiles, dyeing, and fertilizers is a significant concern. These industries release various pollutants, including heavy metals, organic compounds, and chemicals, which are hazardous to human health [1,2]. Among these, organic dyes are a major source of contamination because of their extensive use, vivid colors, slow degradation, and low cost [3,4]. Various conventional methods have been employed to treat these contaminants, including filtration, coagulation, adsorption, membrane filtration, ozonation, reverse osmosis, and photocatalytic degradation. However, each method has limitations, prompting the development of Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs) as a superior approach for eliminating resistant organic pollutants [5]. AOPs rely on generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) like OḢ, O2–, O2, and H2O2 through photochemical, electrochemical, and sono-chemical/catalytic methods, which play a crucial role in breaking down organic contaminants [6,7]. It is necessary to identify and work on local pollutants to come over the pollution problem. Due to increasing population and decreasing agriculture resources the pesticides use is enhancing drastically. Apart from this Aggressive urbanization and industrialization has significantly contributed to the problem of pollution. In past decades, the pollutants released from agriculture and industries such as dyes and pesticides etc., have emerged as great concern. So, it’s need of the hour to focus and bleak the pollutants from industries as well as generated from agriculture. With the aim “to kill two birds with a stone” we are working to develop the multifunctional hybrid nanomaterials. The choice of Rhodamine B and 4-Nitrophenol is well-justified based on their environmental relevance, toxicity, and resistance to conventional treatment methods. The selected pollutants effectively demonstrate the efficacy and multifunctionality of our synthesized CeO2@C-dots nanospheres. We need to identify and work on the region specific pollutants to deal with the problem.

Among various treatment methods, sono-catalytic degradation, using ultrasonic waves, is a particularly promising technique for environmental cleanup. It facilitates the formation of reactive radicals, driven by the cavitation effect, where tiny bubbles accumulate energy and eventually collapse [8]. This collapse creates extreme conditions—temperatures reaching approximately 5000 K and pressures up to 1800 atm—leading to the splitting of water molecules and the formation of highly reactive radicals (OḢ and ̇H). However, relying solely on ultrasound requires significant time and energy. To overcome this limitation, recent research has focused on combining sonocatalysis with heterogeneous catalysts that remain active under ultrasonic irradiation. This combination significantly enhances the efficiency of dye pollutant degradation [9,10].

Cerium oxide (CeO2), known for its ability to switch between oxidation states +4 (Ce4+) and +3 (Ce3+), is a promising heterogeneous catalyst for organic pollutant degradation [11]. Recent advancements in ceria nanostructures have demonstrated the effectiveness of the hydrothermal method for synthesizing CeO2 nanomaterials with well-controlled shapes and sizes at low temperatures. This method offers the advantages of simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and the ability to produce highly reactive nanomaterials [12].

To further improve the catalytic performance of metal oxides, methods like immobilization on supports or doping with transition metals have been explored [13]. Recently, carbon dots (C-dots)- a class of ultrafine nanomaterials (below 10 nm)- have gained attention due to their versatility in applications such as catalysis, drug delivery, and environmental purification. Carbon dots, derived from graphite, green natural materials, polymers, or small molecules, are particularly appealing when synthesized from biomass due to their environmentally friendly and sustainable production process [14,15]. Biowaste-derived carbon nanostructures have gained attention for being easy to prepare and adding value to otherwise discarded waste materials. C-dots are ideal for creating finely dispersed nanostructures due to their excellent fluorescence properties, low cost, chemical stability, low toxicity, and strong photochemical properties [16].

This study focuses on the fabrication of a novel CeO2@C nanocomposite using naturally occurring, affordable, and readily available biowaste, specifically tea waste and chestnut peels. The CeO2 nanoparticles (NPs) and hydrophilic, stable C-dots were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal technique, avoiding harsh chemical reagents. Notably, this study introduces a sustainable and eco-friendly approach for synthesizing CeO2@C-dots nanospheres using agro-biowaste materials (chestnut peels and tea waste) as precursors. This green synthesis method avoids the use of hazardous chemicals, contributing to environmental sustainability while valorizing biowaste into high-performance nanomaterials. Under ultrasonic irradiation, the CeO2@C nanocomposite demonstrated remarkable performance in the adsorption and degradation of Rhodamine B dye. The catalyst's performance was optimized by evaluating factors influencing sono-chemical degradation. Additionally, the CeO2@C nanocomposite served as a highly effective electron mediator in constructing a 4-nitrophenol (4NP) sensor in an aqueous environment. The multifunctionality of the synthesized material, combining pollutant degradation and chemical sensing, is explored and discussed in this work.

All chemicals used in this study were of research-grade and applied without further modification. Cerium (III) nitrate hexahydrate (minimum assay: 99.5 %), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, minimum assay: 97.0 %), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), ethanol (purity ≥ 99.9 %), 4-nitrophenol (minimum assay: 99.0 %), and Rhodamine B (RB) were procured from Central Drug House (CDH), New Delhi. The pH of the solutions was adjusted using 0.1 M HCl and 1.0 M NaOH. Tea waste was collected from local canteens and tea shops, and water chestnuts were sourced from the nearby region of Murthal, Sonipat, Haryana. Double-distilled (DD) water was used preferentially for preparing all solutions.

Carbon dots (C-dots) were synthesized via a cost-effective and eco-friendly method. Initially, 5 g of water chestnut peels were thoroughly washed with water and oven-dried at 120 °C. The dried peel powder was then mixed with 100 mL of sodium hypochlorite solution and left undisturbed for approximately 5 h. Afterward, the mixture was washed multiple times until a neutral pH was achieved. The oxidized peel powder was subjected to hydrothermal treatment in a Teflon-lined autoclave at 180 °C for 10 h. After natural cooling, the resulting brown solution was washed to remove byproduct impurities. The final product was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 20 min, and the precipitates were recovered, washed, and dried at 100 °C. The yield of water-soluble, biowaste-derived carbon dots was calculated based on the initial weight of the chestnut peel (5 g) and the final product weight (0.897 g), resulting in a yield of 17.9 %.

Tea waste collected from nearby canteens and shops was thoroughly washed with an aqueous mixture of ethyl alcohol to remove impurities, followed by drying at 100 °C for five hours. The dried tea waste was ground into powder and passed through a sieve to obtain fine particles. A tea waste extract (TWE) was prepared by mixing the tea waste powder with water in a 1:10 ratio and heating the mixture at 100 °C for six hours with continuous stirring. The resulting TWE was centrifuged, filtered, and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further use.

Next, 80 mL of 0.5 M cerium nitrate solution was slowly mixed with 20 mL of TWE, with continuous stirring at 100 °C for five hours. To this solution, 2–3 drops of 2 M NaOH were added, followed by ultrasonication for 20 min. The color change to pale yellow indicated the formation of CeO2 nanoparticles (NPs). The NPs were separated by centrifugation at 4500 rpm for 10 min and washed with double-distilled water and ethanol.

CeO2@C-dots were synthesized by mixing an aqueous solution of the synthesized CeO2 NPs with carbon dots via ultrasonication in a 10:5 ratio. The resulting mixture was subjected to hydrothermal treatment in a Teflon-lined autoclave at 180 °C for 10 h.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was conducted using a Philips X'celerator detector (PW 3050/60) at a scan rate of 8° per minute. Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å) was employed over a diffraction angle range (2θ) of 10° to 80° to assess the crystallinity of the samples. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was performed using a PerkinElmer Frontier, with the nanocomposite combined with KBr and pelletized for analysis within the 400–4000 cm−1 scan range. The microstructural morphology was analyzed using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, JEOL-JSM-5600LV). Elemental composition and chemical states were determined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) on an ESCALAB 250 Xi system (Thermo Fisher Scientific). Total Organic Carbon (TOC) was analyzed using Shimazdu, TOC-L analyzer.

The sono-catalytic performance of the fabricated material was evaluated for the degradation of Rhodamine B (RB) dye using an ultrasonication technique. Batch experiments were carried out in an ultrasound bath (LABMAN scientific instruments) operating at a frequency of 40 kHz with a power intensity of approximately 1 W cm−2. A Pyrex reaction tube containing the dye solution and catalyst suspension was immersed in the sonicator, ensuring the tube was submerged two-thirds of its height. Before ultrasonic irradiation, 50 mL of dye solution (from a 30 mg/L RB stock solution) with a known concentration of the catalyst was prepared. The solution was magnetically stirred for 30 min in the dark to establish absorption–desorption equilibrium between the catalyst and dye.

The initial pH of the dye solutions was adjusted using 0.1 mol L−1 HCl to create acidic conditions or 0.1 mol L−1 NaOH for alkaline conditions. The residual concentration of Rhodamine B was measured spectrophotometrically. The decolorization efficiency (DE) [17] was calculated using the following equation:
(1)
D E
%
=
C o
- C
C o
X 100

Additionally, the uptake capacity (q) of Rhodamine B was determined using:
(2)
q
mg
g
=
C o
- C
V
m
where:
•
C o
is the initial dye concentration (mg/L),
•
C is the dye concentration at time t (mg/L),
•
C e
represents the equilibrium concentration of dye (mg/L),
•
V is the volume of the solution (L)
•
m is the dry weight of the catalyst (g).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of 4-nitrophenol chemical sensor using current–voltage (I–V) technique
ce:section.ce:para: A 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS) at pH 7.0 was prepared and used for sensing 4-nitrophenol (4NP). The CeO2@C-dots nanospheres were used to modify the surface of a gold electrode (surface area = 3 mm2). The gold electrode surface was first polished using 0.5 μm alumina powder and then cleaned to remove any physically adsorbed substances by rinsing with double-distilled water, followed by ultrasonic treatment. A slurry was prepared by mixing the nanomaterial with a 5 % ethanolic solution of Nafion as a binding agent in a predetermined ratio. This paste was carefully applied to the gold electrode surface and dried in an oven at 60 ± 1°C. The sensitivity of the modified electrode was assessed using an Autolab type-III cyclic voltammeter with a three-electrode system, comprising a working electrode (CeO2@C-dots-modified gold), a reference electrode (Ag/AgCl), and a counter electrode (Pt wire).

Fig. 1 (a (i and ii)) represents the FTIR data of the material prepared. The fundamental stretching bands (Ce–O) of the CeO2 NPs originated at 660 cm−1 and 816 cm−1, representing the corresponding bands ascribed to the metal–oxygen bond. The broad bands around 1050 cm−1 were related to establishing ''carbonate-like'' species on the CeO2 surface [18]. Also, the absorption bands at 1730 cm−1 ascribed the characteristic C = O and C–OH stretching vibrations of the COOH group, respectively, of carbon dots, which were not seen in CeO2 NPs. A broad band in the 2800–3700 cm−1 range could be given to the –OH stretching and deformation vibrations produced by water molecules [19]. Furthermore, some peaks of the CeO2@C-dotsnanosphere have been merged or slightly shifted to a lower wave number, owing to the development of hydrogen bonds between the CeO2 NPs and hydroxyl groups on the surface of the carbon dots. FTIR data of RB dye (before and after treatment)are displayed in Fig. 1 (a (iii and iv)), respectively, which will be discussed in the later section.

The XRD patterns of CeO2 nanoparticles (NPs) and CeO2@C-dots nanospheres are shown in Fig. 1(b). The XRD profile confirmed the crystalline nature of the CeO2 NPs. High-intensity peaks were observed at 28.44°, 33.12°, 47.62°, 58.16°, 60.02°, and 69.66°, corresponding to the (111), (200), (220), (311), (222), and (400) crystal planes, respectively. These planes matched well with the JCPDS reference card No. 34–0394 for CeO2, indicating a pristine cubic fluorite structure. The sharpness of the diffraction peaks reflects the high crystallinity of the synthesized nanoparticles [20]. Additionally, a peak at 22.20° was observed in the CeO2@C-dots sample, attributed to the (002) plane of carbon, confirming the successful formation of CeO2@C-dots nanospheres alongside the CeO2 peaks.

The morphological features of CeO2 and CeO2@C-dots nanospheres were examined using SEM analysis. As shown in Fig. 1(c), CeO2 exhibits a rough, cube-shaped morphology. A significant transformation in the morphology of the CeO2 NPs was observed after the deposition of carbon dots through hydrothermal treatment. This process led to the conversion of CeO2 into hollow spherical shells, with an approximate size range of 100–200 nm, densely coated with carbon dots, as depicted in Fig. 1(d) (the inset shows a clear hollow spherical structure). The rough and porous surfaces of the synthesized CeO2@C-dots composite indicate that these hollow spheres are composed of many small CeO2 NPs, covered with carbon dots.

The typical images of C-dots and CeO2@C-dots nanospheres, dispersed in deionized water, are shown in Fig. 2 , obtained through high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As seen in Fig. 2(a), the synthesized C-dots are uniform black particles with no visible aggregation, having an average size of approximately 3–4 nm. In contrast, the TEM image of CeO2@C-dots reveals irregular, agglomerated hollow spheres, indicating the deposition of C-dots on the surface of CeO2 nanoparticles, as shown in the inset of Fig. 2(b). These findings, corroborated by both SEM and TEM analyses, suggest a uniform distribution of C-dots across the entire surface of the agglomerated CeO2 particles.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to determine the surface atomic composition and valence states of the fabricated material. Fig. 3 (a) shows the survey spectrum of CeO2@C-dots nanospheres, confirming the presence of Ce, O, and C. The Ce 3d spectrum (Fig. 3b) exhibits complexity in the 870–925 eV range, due to the hybridization of the O2p valence band and the Ce 4f level. Peaks labeled as v, v2, v3, u, u2, and u3 correspond to Ce4+ species, while v1 (886.4 eV) and u1 (905.1 eV) are attributed to Ce3+ with oxygen vacancies [21]. This indicates the coexistence of both Ce4+ and Ce3+ ions in the CeO2@C-dots nanospheres.

The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3c) shows asymmetry, which is deconvoluted into two peaks at 529.1 eV and 531.7 eV, corresponding to lattice oxygen (O2−) and oxygen species such as O2 − or OH−. In the C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3d), peaks at 284.3 eV, 285.0 eV, and 288.2 eV indicate the presence of C = C/C–C, C = O, and O–C = O functional groups within the CeO2@C-dots nanospheres [22].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sono-chemical degradation of Rhodamine B dye
ce:section.ce:para: Batch mode investigations were conducted under various conditions, including different adsorbent concentrations, initial dye concentrations relative to time, pH levels, and in the presence of hydroxyl radical scavengers. The UV–Vis spectrum of Rhodamine B (RB) exhibits two distinct absorption peaks in the 400 to 600 nm range: a minor shoulder around 490 nm and a significant broad peak centered at 525 nm, as shown in Fig. 4 (b). The intensity of the absorption peaks for RB progressively decreases under the different parameters and conditions (which will be explained in detail in a later section). After a certain period, the peaks nearly vanish entirely, without the formation of any new peaks. The rapid decline in the absorption peaks is indicative of the disintegration of the chromophore structure, which is primarily responsible for the dye’s color. This observation suggests the effective degradation of the dye over time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Percentage removal of RB dyes under different conditions
ce:section.ce:para: The dye solution was subjected to various testing parameters to evaluate the effect of each on the degradation process. The decomposition of Rhodamine B (RB) dye, with and without direct ultrasonication (US), was examined, and a similar pattern was observed for the prepared nanocomposite CeO2@C-dots (Fig. 4). The rate of dye removal was generally slower in all cases except when ultrasonication was combined with CeO2 nanoparticles (NPs), where the removal of RB dye reached approximately 40 %. This limited degradation can be attributed to the restricted production of hydroxyl radicals due to the splitting of water molecules under sonication. However, when the synergistic effect of carbon dots (C-dots) anchored on CeO2 NPs in an ultrasonic environment was applied, nearly complete mineralization of the RB dye was achieved within 10 min of contact time. This rapid degradation is attributed to the enhanced mass transfer and diffusion rate, which further amplified the acoustic cavitation effect, significantly boosting the efficiency of the process [5].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of catalyst dosage and dye concentrations
ce:section.ce:para: The degradation rate of Rhodamine B (RB) dye is significantly influenced by both catalyst loading and the initial dye concentration [23]. Catalyst dosages ranging from 0.05, 0.08, 0.1, 0.2, and 0.3 g, along with dye concentrations of 30, 50, and 80 mg/L, were used to perform the dye degradation experiments. As shown in Fig. 5 (a), a catalyst dosage of 0.2 g of CeO2@C-dots combined with an initial RB concentration of 30 mg/L resulted in nearly complete mineralization of ∼ 99 % within 10 min. Increasing the catalyst dosage beyond 0.2 g did not produce significant changes in the degradation rate, leading to the optimization of 0.2 g as the fixed catalyst dosage for subsequent experiments. As discussed, hydroxyl radicals are the primary oxidizing agents responsible for the degradation of RB dye in this process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on sono-chemical degradation efficiency
ce:section.ce:para: pH is a crucial parameter that was thoroughly investigated to evaluate the dye removal efficiency. Various pH levels (4, 6, 8, and 9) were prepared to assess the impact of pH on the degradation of Rhodamine B (RB) at an initial dye concentration of 30 mg/L, over 10 min, and using an optimal catalyst dosage of 0.2 g (refer to Fig. 5b). The adsorption and degradation efficiency showed a significant dependence on the removal rate at different pH values. The sono-degradation process occurred more rapidly in alkaline environments compared to neutral or acidic conditions. Among the pH levels tested, pH 9 exhibited the highest removal of RB, making it the most favorable and therefore optimized. As indicated in Fig. 5(b), the acidic environment, which has a higher concentration of hydronium ions (H+), was less effective for RB removal. At low pH, the adsorption of the cationic RB molecule was hindered due to the electrostatic repulsion between the positively charged dye and the similarly charged catalyst surface [24]. In contrast, in alkaline conditions, the RB dye demonstrated improved adsorption due to the attraction between the cationic –NH2 + groups of the RB molecule and the negatively charged surface of the catalyst, leading to its enhanced degradation.

It is essential to investigate the removal rate of Rhodamine B (RB) dye in the presence of salts typically found in industrial wastewater, which are often referred to as scavengers, to assess the potential application of the process [25]. These radical scavengers were employed in this study to trap and neutralize reactive species involved in the sono-catalytic decolorization of RB dye. As shown in Fig. 5(c), the addition of salts such as NaCl, Na2SO4, and NaHCO3 with 0.1 M, significantly reduced the dye degradation rate by approximately 50 %, 70 %, 65 %, and 75 %, respectively in 10 min.

This reduction occurs because the anions in these salts act as scavengers for hydroxyl radicals, converting them into less efficient anionic radicals, thereby diminishing the overall degradation efficiency. The following equations illustrate this process:
Cl−+⋅OH→Cl⋅−+OH−
SO4 2−+⋅OH→ SO4 ⋅−+OH−
CO
3
2 -
+
.
O H → C
O 3
. -
+ O
H -
C
H 3
2
S O +
.
O H → C
H 3
S
O 2
H + C
H 3 .
To understand the role of hydroxyl (•OH) radicals in the degradation process, experiments were conducted by varying the concentration of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) from 0 to 1 M. A significant suppression in the degradation rate exceeding 50 % ffwas observed as the concentration of DMSO increased, as shown in Fig. 5(c). This marked decrease in efficiency suggests that •OH radicals play a crucial role in the degradation of Rhodamine B (RB). The results confirm that the attack by hydroxyl radicals is the dominant reaction mechanism in the ultrasonication-driven degradation of RB dye.

A kinetic study of the sono-degradation of Rhodamine B (RB) dye was conducted at initial concentrations of 30, 50, and 80 mg/L. The characteristic constants were determined using a pseudo-first-order equation proposed by Lagergren, which is based on the solid's adsorption capacity to investigate the degradation mechanism. The equation is described as follows [26]:
ln
C
0
C
= k t

In the equation, k (min−1) represents the rate constant, while C0 and C denote the initial and subjected dye concentrations at time t, respectively. Linear plots were obtained when ln(C0/C) was plotted against t for all the concentrations studied (Fig. 5(d)). The results from the kinetic studies were analyzed based on the disappearance of the dye color, as observed through UV–visible spectroscopy. As presented in Table 1 , the degradation of Rhodamine B (RB) dye follows a pseudo-first-order kinetic reaction.

The comparative analysis highlights the efficacy of metal oxide/carbon dots-based green nanocomposites in dye removal. The influence of green precursors on removal efficiency is evident, future research should focus on scaling up the synthesis of these nanocomposites, improving their stability and reusability, and exploring their potential applications in real-world wastewater treatment scenarios.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Plausible mechanism of RB dye degradation
ce:section.ce:para:
Rhodamine B (RB) dye tends to remain stable even when exposed to irradiated photons in environments containing catalysts, oxidants, or additives [27]. However, ultrasonic-assisted degradation of RB, in synergy with CeO2@C-dots nanospheres, led to N-de-ethylation of the dye by generating nitrogen-centered radicals through the breakdown of its chromophore structures. Ultrasonically generated active species, such as hydroxyl radicals (OH*) and superoxide radicals (*O2 –) formed by the reaction between generated electrons and the surface adsorbed oxygen, target the core carbon atoms of the RB dye, initiating its degradation [37]. The extreme conditions created during the sono-assisted experiments cause water molecules to split into free radicals, further enhancing the degradation process.
H 2
O
→
〉 〉 〉 U S
O
H ∙
+
H ∙

The hotspot formations at an extremely high temperature (∼105) are caused by ultrasonic cavitation to generate e- (electron) and H+ (protons) pairs on the surface of catalysts (CeO2@C-dots nanosphere).

The electron (e−) and proton (H+) pair may either recombine or react with water and surface adsorbed oxygen to generate hydroxyl and superoxide radicals after cooling. However, the presence of C-dots on the CeO2 nanoparticles serves two crucial purposes: first, their surface chemistry, which is typically rich in various functional groups, ensures rapid adsorption of the dye; second, the C-dots act as electron scavengers, suppressing the recombination of e− and H+. This suppression favours the production of additional hydroxyl radicals and superoxide radicals, which significantly contribute to the degradation of dye molecules[38]. The proposed mechanism is shown in Fig. 6 .
During dye degradation (in the presence of CeO2@C-dots),OH. radicals formed in the above equations directed the decomposition of the RB dye to the final product, i.e., CO2 and H2O [25,27]:

According to literature survey, the breakdown of RB occurs via the following pathways: chromophore breakage, decarboxylation, N-demethylation/N-de-ethylation, and ultimate mineralization. The change in RB's absorption spectra during sonocatalysis (using 30 mg/l of dye concentration, dosage of 0.2 g and pH 9) was acknowledged as a substantial piece of evidence for putting forward the mechanisms of degradation. Scheme 1 shows that a hypsochromic shift occurred with increasing sonolysis time, simultaneous with a decrease in the absorption band at 525 nm. At 10 min of sonocatalysis time, the maximum absorption wavelength shifted from 525 to 512 nm. In the sonochemical degradation of RhB, a decrease in the absorption band at 525 nm may be attributed to chromophore group cleavage, while the blue hypsochromic shift of the peaks are associated with N-deethylation process [39]. These findings suggest that the N-deethylation (pathway I) and the cleavage of the aromatic chromophore ring structure (pathway II) occur during the sonolysis of RB, but the breakdown of the aromatic structure predominates. The released active radical ions target the N-ethyl at the nitrogen site for cleavage. This occurs due to the p-type electron orbital of nitrogen interacting with the p-type electron orbital of the benzene ring, creating a conjugated system. Several studies have verified the presence of N-de-ethylation using the LC-ESI-MS/MS method [4041]. Furthermore, N-de-ethylated intermediates are also attacked by active radicals resulting in the formation of several primary oxidation products, such as benzyloxyamine, benzoic acid and phthalic acid [41]. Consequently, the processes of aromatic chromophore clevage and N-ethyl dissociation took place concurrently throughout the reaction.

The measured values of TOC removed from RB solutions after 3, 6 and 10 min was analyzed and found to be 33.5, 58.7 and 72.6 %, respectively (Detailed discussion and Fig. S1 in Supplementary data file). The outcomes demonstrate that on increasing ultrasonication irradiation time and in the presence of CeO2@C-dots nanospheres, the RB dye illustrated good effectively mineralization efficacy.

The FTIR data shows the removal of Rhodamine B (RB) dye before and after ultrasonic treatment, as depicted in Fig. 1a (iii and iv). The FTIR spectrum of pure RB dye exhibited bands in the 1500–500 cm−1 range, corresponding to di-substituted and tri-substituted benzene rings. Additionally, the C–H (CH2) stretching band at approximately 2900 cm−1, the C-O vibrational stretching at 1780 cm−1, and aromatic skeletal vibrations at 1475 and 1390 cm−1 confirmed the expected structure [42]. After sono-degradation of the dye, the intensity of these peaks either decreased, disappeared or shifted, indicating the breakdown of the aromatic rings and other mineralized products (Fig. 1a (iv)). The stretching vibrational peak of the carbonyl group at 1780 cm−1 significantly diminished, likely due to the formation of other carbonyl-containing groups, such as carboxylic acids and aldehydes. Peaks around 2900 cm−1, assigned to aromatic and alkenyl groups, also disappeared after treatment, suggesting complete breakdown. The degradation process showed significant changes in the bands within the 500–1500 cm−1 range. The characteristic band around 1400 cm−1, attributed to the C = C bonds in the benzene ring, shifted, broadened, and decreased in intensity following 10 min of ultrasonic treatment with CeO2@C-dots.

The CeO2@C-dots nanospheres were recycled and reused three times with little to no loss in decolorization efficiency. The catalyst underwent several processes, including centrifugation and repeated washing with ethanol and water, followed by drying at 100 °C for two hours. The optimized catalyst was reused across three cycles, with observed degradation efficiencies of 96.3 %, 94.7 %, and 91.6 % after the 1st, 2nd, and 3rd cycles, respectively. The slight decrease in efficiency can be attributed to the loss of catalyst during the washing and centrifugation steps. These results indicate that CeO2@C-dots exhibit good stability and reusability after three consecutive cycles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performance of prepared CeO2@C-dotsnanospheremodified Au electrode towards 4- nitrophenol
ce:section.ce:para: In the studies, a neutral phosphate buffer solution was used for the measurement of 4-nitrophenol (4NP) using the CeO2@C-dots nanosphere-modified electrochemical sensor during cyclic voltammetry (CV) (Fig. 7 (a)). To determine the electro-catalytic activity of CeO2@C-dots toward the electrochemical response of 4NP, CV measurements were recorded with and without 4NP in the buffer solution. As shown in Fig. 7(b), no significant variation in current magnitude was observed in the absence of 4NP. However, in the presence of 4NP, the CV graph demonstrated enhanced electrochemical behavior, likely due to the excellent conductivity and large surface area of the CeO2@C-dots hollow nanospheres [43]. The CV measurements revealed an irreversible reduction peak at a potential of −0.504 V with a current (Ip) of −5.66 µA. These results suggest that the CeO2@C-dots modified gold electrode can efficiently function as an excellent electron mediator, making it a promising candidate for fabricating electrochemical sensors.

The scan rate significantly influenced the sensing activity of the modified electrode and was investigated through cyclic voltammetry (CV) across a range of scan rates (20, 50, 100, 200, 300, 500, 600, 800, and 1000 mV/s). Fig. 8 illustrates the typical CV curves obtained for the CeO2@C-dots modified gold electrode at different scan rates in a 0.1 M phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution. The current values (Ip) of the reduction peak increased with rising scan rates and shifted toward more negative potentials. This behaviour suggests that the reduction process on the electrode surface is diffusion-controlled and quasi-reversible [44]. Fig. 6(b) further shows the relationship between the anodic peak current and the square root of the scan rate (v1/2). The linearity of this relationship indicates that the current is directly proportional to the square root of the scan rate, reflecting that diffusion controls the kinetics of the overall electrochemical process.13.

A detailed investigation of the electrochemical sensing of 4-nitrophenol (4NP) was conducted using amperometric studies. A series of cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were performed with the CeO2@C-dots modified gold electrode at concentrations ranging from 10 µM to 100 µM, using a constant potential of −0.504 V in a 0.1 M PBS solution (pH = 7). As shown in Fig. 8, the consecutive addition of 4NP resulted in a significant increase in current response. The rise in peak current upon adding 4NP indicates that the fabricated electrode exhibits a sensitive and rapid response to 4NP. A corresponding calibration curve was plotted between steady-state current and 4NP concentration, showing a linear relationship with a correlation coefficient of 0.9157 (inset Fig. 9
).

The sensor's sensitivity for 4NP was calculated using a widely accepted method, where the slope of the calibration curve is divided by the electrode's surface area. The detection limit of the sensor was determined using the following equation:
Detection
Limit =
3 ×
n o i s e
Slope
of
the
calibration
curve
The calculated sensitivity and detection limit were found to be 43.4 µA/µMcm2 and 0.94 µM, respectively.

The current response in the I-V (current–voltage) technique during 4-nitrophenol (4NP) sensing was significantly influenced by the morphology, porosity, and dimensions of the CeO2@C-dots nanospheres. When the nanocomposite-modified Au electrode surface comes into contact with 4NP, a redox reaction occurs, generating a prominent reduction peak corresponding to 4-hydroxyaminophenol. Subsequently, a pair of redox peaks in the cyclic voltammetry (CV) graph indicates the oxidation process of 4-hydroxyaminophenol, leading to the formation of 4-nitrophenol [45]. This study suggests that the CeO2@C-dots nanosphere-modified Au electrode effectively adsorbs 4NP molecules and undergoes a redox reaction, as confirmed by the enhanced current response with applied potential.

The performance of the synthesized electrochemical sensor was compared to other studies, as shown in Table 3 , and the lowest detection limit was achieved and reported [44,46–49]. The CeO2@C-dots-based chemical sensor nanospheres exhibited good reproducibility, remaining effective for up to one month without any significant shift in potential or peak current when stored under controlled temperature conditions in an air-tight container. Despite its distinctive redox characteristics, CeO2 has restricted structural stability and conductivity. Importantly, when Ce4+ is reduced to Ce3+, there is a notable expansion of the lattice structure [13]. Refer to Fig. S2 (in Supplementary data file), the composite demonstrates strong cycle stability up to 1000 cycles, with a slight decrease noted after 1000 cycles due to the transition between the Ce3+ and Ce4+ oxidation states.

In this study, we have successfully synthesized CeO2@C-dots nanospheres using an eco-friendly and sustainable hydrothermal approach from biowaste-derived precursors. The material demonstrated exceptional multifunctionality, combining efficient sono-catalytic degradation of Rhodamine B (RB) dye and electrochemical sensing of 4-Nitrophenol (4NP). The sono-catalytic system achieved nearly 99 % RB dye degradation within 10 min, facilitated by the synergistic interaction between CeO2 nanoparticles and carbon dots, which enhanced electron-hole pair separation, increased surface reactivity, and optimized reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation. The electrochemical sensor based on CeO2@C-dots exhibited remarkable performance with a sensitivity of 43.4 μA/μMcm2 and a detection limit of 0.94 μM for 4NP, demonstrating its suitability for real-time environmental monitoring. Furthermore, the reusability and stability tests confirmed that the CeO2@C-dots nanospheres maintained their catalytic and sensing efficiency over multiple cycles, underscoring their robustness and potential for practical applications. This work not only highlights the dual capability of CeO2@C-dots nanospheres in addressing critical environmental challenges but also emphasizes the broader significance of utilizing agro-waste as a resource for high-value functional nanomaterials. Future studies should focus on scaling up the synthesis process, assessing real wastewater samples, and evaluating long-term toxicity impacts to pave the way for commercialization and widespread adoption in environmental remediation technologies.

Our findings provide a significant step forward in green nanotechnology and showcase how biowaste-derived functional materials can offer sustainable and scalable solutions for environmental pollution mitigation and monitoring.

Vinit Kumar: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Indu Kaushal: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Ashok K. Sharma: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision. Sourabh Sharma: Writing – original draft, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis. Ashok Kumar: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology. Santosh Bhukal: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation. Jyoti Rani: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Resources. Priya Saharan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by DST-INSPIRE Faculty fellowship, India (Project Grant no. DST/INSPIRE /04/2021/003328).
